name: Mortar Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Mortar. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for mortar cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for mortar absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise mortar cleaning with adequate energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient mortar removal with good surface quality

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective mortar ablation without damaging underlying substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective mortar ablation with controlled thermal effects

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform mortar removal and processing efficiency

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete mortar removal with quality control

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform mortar removal and surface consistency

power.description: Typical range for industrial 1064 nm laser cleaning of mortar-like cementitious materials to remove contaminants without excessive heat buildup; starting at 500 W for controlled ablation. Max avoids substrate cracking (thermal conductivity ~1 W/mK). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on stone cleaning (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance removal efficiency and heat input; slower for heavy contamination (e.g., paint), faster for light dirt. Recommended 1500 mm/s for uniform mortar surface prep. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of mortar; 30 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to mortar's low thermal diffusivity (~0.5 mm²/s). Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond optional for precision but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015) on ablation of cementitious substrates.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed 1064 nm systems on mortar to achieve pulse overlap without excessive heat accumulation; higher rates speed up cleaning but risk thermal damage on porous mortar. 50 kHz balances speed and safety. Source: Academic literature, e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on stone heritage cleaning trade-offs.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation on mortar; below damage threshold (~3-5 J/cm² for cement, avoiding melting point ~1200°C). Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe removal of oxides/paint with minimal porosity alteration. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Cult. Herit. (2017) on laser cleaning of masonry.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for industrial cleaning; smaller for fine details, larger for broad mortar surfaces. 500 μm recommended for efficient coverage on rough mortar textures. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning heads.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass for heavy layers on mortar to prevent over-ablation. Recommended 2 passes for complete removal while preserving substrate integrity. Source: Industry case studies, e.g., restoration projects in Constr. Build. Mater. (2020).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage on irregular mortar surfaces; 50% optimal for even energy distribution without gaps or hotspots. Higher for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization in Laser Technol. (2016) for surface preparation applications.
